channel.title: Implicit-Any on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Implicit-Any on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Implicit Any Type — Expression Has No Index Signature Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Element implicitly has an ‘any’ type because expression of type ‘X’ is not of type ‘Y’

This error occurs when you access a property on an object using a dynamic key, but the object’s type doesn’t have an index signature that allows that key. TypeScript cannot guarantee the key exists, so it would implicitly be any.

Common Causes

  • Dynamic property access without index signature — obj[dynamicKey] where obj has no index signature
  • Using a variable as a key on a typed object — record[key] where key isn’t a known literal
  • Enum used as object key — obj[enumValue] where the object type doesn’t include the enum
  • Dot notation with dynamic string — accessing properties that aren’t declared in the interface

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Dynamic access on typed object
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const field = "name";
console.log(user[field]); // TS7053: Element implicitly has an 'any' type

// Fix 1a: Add index signature
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 [key: string]: string | number;
}

// Fix 1b: Use type assertion
console.log(user[field as keyof User]);

// Cause 2: Record access without proper typing
const scores: Record<string, number> = { math: 90, science: 85 };
const key = "math";
console.log(scores[key]); // this works because Record has an index signature

// Cause 3: Enum as key
enum Color { Red = "red", Blue = "blue" }
const palette: Record<Color, string> = {
 [Color.Red]: "#ff0000",
 [Color.Blue]: "#0000ff",
};

const c = Color.Red;
console.log(palette[c]); // this works with Record<Color, string>

Examples

// Example 1: Object literal without index signature
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}

const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
const keys: string[] = ["host", "port"];

keys.forEach(key => {
 console.log(config[key]); // TS7053
});

// Fix: use keyof assertion
keys.forEach(key => {
 console.log(config[key as keyof Config]);
});

// Example 2: Nested dynamic access
interface Nested {
 a: { b: { c: number } };
}

const obj: Nested = { a: { b: { c: 42 } } };
const path = ["a", "b", "c"];

// TypeScript can't verify this path is valid
path.reduce((acc, key) => acc[key], obj); // TS7053

// Fix: use type assertion
path.reduce((acc: any, key) => acc[key], obj) as any;

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter has no type annotation — implicit any on parameters
  • TS7031: Binding element has implicit any — destructured parameters without types
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — static access to non-existent property

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2683 — 'this' Implicitly Has Type 'any'
item.description:
TS2683 — ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’

TS2683 occurs when noImplicitThis is enabled (part of strict mode) and TypeScript can’t determine the type of this in a function. This commonly happens with standalone functions, callbacks, and untyped method references.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Standalone function without 'this' parameter
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683: 'this' implicitly has type 'any'
}

// Cause 2: Callback losing 'this' context
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++; // TS2683: 'this' is 'any' inside callback
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Cause 3: Passing method as callback without binding
class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const fn = new Logger().log;
fn(); // TS2683: 'this' is 'any'

// Cause 4: Object literal with untyped 'this'
const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue() {
 return this.value; // TS2683 if noImplicitThis is on
 }
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a ’this’ parameter to the function

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name);
}

// Correct
interface ThisContext {
 name: string;
}
function greet(this: ThisContext) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Fix 2: Use arrow functions to preserve ’this'

// Wrong
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Correct — arrow function captures 'this' lexically
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(() => {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

Fix 3: Use .bind() when passing methods as callbacks

class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const logger = new Logger();
const fn = logger.log.bind(logger);
fn();

Fix 4: Type ’this’ in arrow function properties

const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue: function (this: { value: number }) {
 return this.value;
 }
};

Examples

// Example 1: jQuery-style callback
$(".button").on("click", function () {
 $(this).addClass("active"); // TS2683: 'this' is HTMLElement, not any
 // Fix: add 'this: HTMLElement' parameter
});

// Example 2: Event handler
document.addEventListener("click", function (e) {
 console.log(this.id); // TS2683: 'this' is Document, not HTMLElement
});

Related Errors

  • TS2684: The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable — ’this’ type mismatch.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — other implicit any issues.
  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured implicit any.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2683 — Implicitly has type 'any'
item.description:
TS2683 — Implicitly has type ‘any’

TS2683 occurs when noImplicitThis is enabled and TypeScript cannot determine the type of this in a function. Without explicit typing, this defaults to any.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Regular function without this type
function logName() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683: 'this' implicitly has type 'any'
}

// Cause 2: Callback losing context
class Person {
 name = "Alice";
 greet() {
 setTimeout(function() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683
 }, 100);
 }
}

// Cause 3: Object method without explicit this
const obj = {
 value: 42,
 getValue() {
 return this.value; // TS2683
 }
};

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use arrow functions

class Person {
 name = "Alice";
 greet() {
 setTimeout(() => {
 console.log(this.name); // OK — arrow function inherits this
 }, 100);
 }
}

Fix 2: Annotate ’this’ parameter

function logName(this: { name: string }) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Fix 3: Use explicitly typed this

interface Loggable {
 name: string;
}
function logName(this: Loggable) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — parameter implicit any.
  • TS2684: The ’this’ context of type is not assignable — this type mismatch.
  • TS18046: Variable is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 — Parameter 'X' implicitly has 'any' type
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter ‘X’ implicitly has ‘any’ type

TS7006 occurs when noImplicitAny is enabled and TypeScript cannot infer a parameter’s type, defaulting it to any. This is a code quality flag — explicit types make code safer and more maintainable.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Callback without type annotation
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2); // TS7006 if noImplicitAny is on

// Cause 2: Event handler without type
element.addEventListener("click", event => {
 console.log(event.target); // TS7006
});

// Cause 3: Unannotated function parameter
function add(a, b) {
 return a + b; // TS7006 for both a and b
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add explicit type annotations

[1, 2, 3].map((x: number) => x * 2);

element.addEventListener("click", (event: MouseEvent) => {
 console.log(event.target);
});

function add(a: number, b: number): number {
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Use type inference from context

// TypeScript infers 'e' from addEventListener
element.addEventListener("click", (e) => {
 console.log(e.clientX); // OK — 'e' is inferred as MouseEvent
});

Fix 3: Disable noImplicitAny (not recommended)

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "noImplicitAny": false
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2683: Implicitly has type ‘any’ — this context implicit any.
  • TS18046: Variable is of type ‘unknown’ — unknown type usage.
  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructuring variant.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 — Parameter Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7006 occurs when noImplicitAny is enabled (part of strict mode) and a function parameter has no type annotation and TypeScript can’t infer its type. This forces you to explicitly declare types for all parameters.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Untyped function parameter
function add(a, b) { // TS7006: Parameter 'a' implicitly has an 'any' type
 return a + b;
}

// Cause 2: Callback without type annotations
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2); // TS7006 if strict mode is on

// Cause 3: Event handler without type
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => { // TS7006: event implicitly has 'any'
 console.log(event.target);
});

// Cause 4: Destructured parameters without types
function process({ name, age }) { // TS7006
 console.log(name, age);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add explicit type annotations

// Wrong
function add(a, b) {
 return a + b;
}

// Correct
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Type callback parameters

// Wrong
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2);

// Correct — TypeScript can usually infer this from the array type
// But if needed:
[1, 2, 3].map((x: number) => x * 2);

Fix 3: Use typed DOM event handlers

// Wrong
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => {});

// Correct
document.addEventListener("click", (event: MouseEvent) => {});

Fix 4: Type destructured parameters

// Wrong
function process({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
interface UserData {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
function process({ name, age }: UserData) {}

Examples

// Example 1: Arrow function in array method
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2); // TS7006: 'n' implicitly has 'any'

// Example 2: Event listener
button.addEventListener("click", e => {
 e.target; // TS7006
});

// Fix: button.addEventListener("click", (e: MouseEvent) => { ... });

Related Errors

  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured parameters.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — type not imported.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — strict typing with unknown.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 v2 — Parameter Implicitly Has Any Type Fix
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter Implicitly Has Any Type (v2)

This variant covers TS7006 errors in callback chains, event handlers, higher-order functions, and destructured parameters where the implicit any is harder to infer or annotate.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS7006: Parameter 'e' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'err' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'item' implicitly has an 'any' type
  • TS7006: Parameter 'data' implicitly has an 'any' type

When noImplicitAny is enabled (recommended), TypeScript requires all parameters to have explicit types. This catches many runtime errors at compile time.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7031 — Binding Element Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7031 — Binding element ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7031 occurs when destructured parameters in a function don’t have type annotations and noImplicitAny is enabled. This is similar to TS7006 but specifically targets destructured binding elements.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Destructured function parameter without types
function greet({ name, age }) { // TS7031: 'name' implicitly has 'any' type
 return `Hello ${name}, you are ${age}`;
}

// Cause 2: Destructured in arrow functions
const process = ({ id, label }) => { // TS7031
 console.log(id, label);
};

// Cause 3: Destructured in method parameters
class Service {
 handle({ data, status }) { // TS7031
 return data;
 }
}

// Cause 4: Destructured with defaults but no type
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) { // TS7031
 console.log(host, port);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Annotate the destructured parameter with an interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: User) {}

Fix 2: Use inline type annotations

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: { name: string; age: number }) {}

Fix 3: Type destructured defaults

// Wrong
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) {}

// Correct
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }: { host?: string; port?: number }) {}

Fix 4: Type arrow function parameters

// Wrong
const process = ({ id, label }) => {};

// Correct
const process = ({ id, label }: { id: number; label: string }) => {};

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }) { // TS7031
 return <button onClick={onClick} disabled={disabled}>{label}</button>;
}

// Fix: type the props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 disabled?: boolean;
}
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }: ButtonProps) {}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — non-destructured parameters.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — related to object shape.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.
